text: The surgical team uses three robotic trocars and two assistant trocars, positioning the robot in a cytokine setup. They target a type III hiatal hernia with a biased hiatal orifice, beginning dissection of the hernia sacs by releasing all attachments using monopolar hooks.
The surgeon uses monopolar hooks to dry the herniated sacs, following literature recommendations, and releases the inter-thoracic ties to free the hernia from surrounding attachments.

The surgeon identifies a lipoma within the hernia sacs and approaches the flaccid part of the small epiblot. The right and left pillars of the diaphragm are completely released, creating a retroesophageal window for further dissection.
The surgeon sutures the retroesophageal area using non-resolvable and braided threads, reducing the size of the hiatal hole. The esophagus is visibly repositioned into an intra-abdominal location.

The surgeon measures the size of the hiatal hole and reinforces it with a pre-cut bioprothesis based on collagen. Monofilament threads are used to fix the bioprothesis at a slow speed with separate points on the diaphragm and pillars. The hiatal orifice is visibly reinforced by the bioprothesis.

The surgeon creates a proximal gastric pouch using an endogea stapler with violet cartridges, preparing for the gastric bypass. The gestational anus is checked and sectioned. Endocyanin green dye and fluorescence imaging are used to assess vascularization and tissue quality during the restoration of digestive continuity at the gastro-gestational anastomosis.

The surgeon opens the proximal stomach and genome using robotic instruments, employing cracked wires at a slow speed to perform the suturing. The robotic instruments provide seven degrees of movement to facilitate precise suturing.

The surgeon calibrates the gastro-regional anastomosis using a nasogastric probe, measuring the anastomosis to be fifteen millimeters in diameter. A food limb of one hundred and fifty centimeters is created, followed by an anastomosis at the limb's base using a GIA endo stapler with forty-five millimeter brown staples. The mechanical clamp holes are closed, and the mesenteric gaps and Petersen's space are sutured with non-resolvable braided thread.
The final view shows the completed gastric bypass with hiatal hernia repair, reinforced by a bioprothesis. The surgical site appears stable, with no visible complications.
